name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَن تُغْنِىَ عَنْهُمْ أَمْوَٰلُهُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمْ وَقُودُ ٱلنَّارِ (١٠)
```

```english
As for the faithless, neither their wealth nor their children shall avail them anything against Allah; it is they who will be fuel for the Fire; (10)
```

```arabic
كَدَأْبِ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (١١)
```

```english
as in the case of Pharaoh’s clan and those who were before them, who denied Our signs. So Allah seized them for their sins, and Allah is severe in retribution. (11)
```

```arabic
قُل لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ سَتُغْلَبُونَ وَتُحْشَرُونَ إِلَىٰ جَهَنَّمَ ۚ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمِهَادُ (١٢)
```

```english
Say to the faithless, ‘You shall be overcome and mustered toward hell, and it is an evil resting place.’ (12)
```

```arabic
قَدْ كَانَ لَكُمْ ءَايَةٌ فِى فِئَتَيْنِ ٱلْتَقَتَا ۖ فِئَةٌ تُقَـٰتِلُ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَأُخْرَىٰ كَافِرَةٌ يَرَوْنَهُم مِّثْلَيْهِمْ رَأْىَ ٱلْعَيْنِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يُؤَيِّدُ بِنَصْرِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَعِبْرَةً لِّأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرِ (١٣)
```

```english
There was certainly a sign for you in the two hosts that met: one host fighting in the way of Allah and the other faithless, who saw them as visibly twice as many. Allah strengthens whomever He wishes with His help. There is indeed a moral in that for those who have insight. (13)
```

```arabic
زُيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ حُبُّ ٱلشَّهَوَٰتِ مِنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ وَٱلْبَنِينَ وَٱلْقَنَـٰطِيرِ ٱلْمُقَنطَرَةِ مِنَ ٱلذَّهَبِ وَٱلْفِضَّةِ وَٱلْخَيْلِ ٱلْمُسَوَّمَةِ وَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ وَٱلْحَرْثِ ۗ ذَٰلِكَ مَتَـٰعُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَٱللَّهُ عِندَهُۥ حُسْنُ ٱلْمَـَٔابِ (١٤)
```

```english
The love of [worldly] allures, including women and children, accumulated piles of gold and silver, horses of mark, livestock, and farms has been made to seem decorous to mankind. Those are the wares of the life of this world, but the goodness of one’s ultimate destination lies near Allah. (14)
```

```arabic
۞ قُلْ أَؤُنَبِّئُكُم بِخَيْرٍ مِّن ذَٰلِكُمْ ۚ لِلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ جَنَّـٰتٌ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا وَأَزْوَٰجٌ مُّطَهَّرَةٌ وَرِضْوَٰنٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بَصِيرٌۢ بِٱلْعِبَادِ (١٥)
```

```english
Say, ‘Shall I inform you of something better than that? For those who are Godwary there will be gardens near their Lord, with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever], and chaste mates, and Allah’s pleasure.’ And Allah watches His servants. (15)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَآ إِنَّنَآ ءَامَنَّا فَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا ذُنُوبَنَا وَقِنَا عَذَابَ ٱلنَّارِ (١٦)
```

```english
Those who say, ‘Our Lord! Indeed, we have faith. So forgive us our sins, and save us from the punishment of the Fire.’ (16)
```

```arabic
ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ وَٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ وَٱلْقَـٰنِتِينَ وَٱلْمُنفِقِينَ وَٱلْمُسْتَغْفِرِينَ بِٱلْأَسْحَارِ (١٧)
```

```english
[They are] patient and truthful, obedient and charitable, and they plead for [Allah’s] forgiveness at dawns. (17)
```

```arabic
شَهِدَ ٱللَّهُ أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ وَأُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمِ قَآئِمًۢا بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۚ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (١٨)
```

```english
Allah, maintainer of justice, the Almighty and the All-wise, besides whom there is no god, bears witness that there is no god except Him, and [so do] the angels and those who possess knowledge. (18)
```

## (Commentary)

It has been previously mentioned that at the time this Surah was revealed, the Muslims were afflicted internally by the hypocrites and others who lent them an ear, as well as by the inclinations and whisperings cast toward them by the enemies of Islam to subvert their affairs and corrupt their mission. Externally, they were afflicted by the world rising against them, with the polytheists, Jews, and Christians mobilizing to nullify their call, extinguish their fire, and put out their light by any means possible, whether by tongue or hand. The purpose of the Surah was to call them to unity of word, patience, and steadfastness, so that their affairs might be rectified and the corruption arising within their atmosphere—as well as that which assailed them from without—might be severed.

The preceding verses—namely, His saying, the Exalted: **هو الذي أنزل عليك الكتاب** (It is He who has sent down to you the Book) until His saying: **إن الله لا يخلف الميعاد** (Indeed Allah does not break His promise)—served as an allusion to the hypocrites and those with deviance in their hearts, and as a call to the Muslims to remain firm in what they understood of the religious sciences (معارف الدين). They were urged to submit and believe in what appeared ambiguous to them and whose core essence and reality they did not fully grasp, by warning that the worst thing that corrupts the matter of religion and drags Muslims into strife (الفتنة) and the breakdown of the system of felicity (نظام السعادة) is the following of the metaphorical verses (المتشابهات) and the seeking of their hidden interpretation (التأويل). Through this, religious guidance is transformed into error and misguidance, and unity is exchanged for division and dispersal.

Then, these verses turn to the state of the disbelievers (الكفار) and the polytheists (المشركين), stating that they shall be overcome; they can neither frustrate Allah, the Glorified, nor succeed in their rebellion. The verses point out that what necessitated their misguidance and confusion was the adornment of worldly desires for them. They presumed, by virtue of the wealth and children they were granted, that these would avail them anything against Allah, the Glorified. They were mistaken in their presumption, for Allah, the Glorified, is the Prevailing in His affair. Had wealth, children, and the like been of any avail against Allah, they would have availed the House of Pharaoh and the oppressive nations before them, who possessed great might and power. Yet, these things availed them nothing, and Allah seized them for their sins. Likewise, these people shall be overcome and seized. Thus, it is obligatory upon the believers to be God-conscious (يتقوا الله) regarding these desires, so that they may attain thereby the felicity of this world and the reward of the Hereafter and the pleasure of their Lord, the Glorified.

As their contents indicate, these verses address the state of the disbelievers, just as the verses following them address the state of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) among the Jews and Christians, as will be discussed.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إن الذين كفروا لن تغني عنهم أموالهم ولا أولادهم من الله شيئاً** (Indeed the possessions and children of the faithless will not avail them anything against Allah). To say "his wealth availed (أغنى) him against so-and-so" means it provided him with sufficiency and removed his need, such that he no longer required him. In the beginning of his formation and consciousness, man perceives himself as needy of what is external to him; this is the first of his primordial knowledge (علمه الفطري) regarding his need for the Designing Creator (الصانع المدبر). Then, as he becomes immersed in the world of means (الأسباب) and senses his needs, he begins by sensing the need for his physical-vegetative perfection (كماله البدني النباتي), which is food and offspring. Subsequently, his soul recognizes other animalistic perfections (الكمالات الحيوانية), which the imagination (الخيال) adorns for him from the trappings of the world—such as the beauty of clothing, dwellings, marriages, and so forth. At this point, the quest for food is replaced by the quest for wealth (المال), which he imagines to be the key to solving all life's problems, as the prevailing custom follows this course. Thus, he imagines that the felicity of his life lies in wealth and children, after having previously thought that the guarantor of his felicity was food and offspring. Then, the soul’s devotion to its desires and the restriction of its concern to the means (الأسباب) causes the heart to stop at these means and grant them independence (الاستقلال). At that moment, he forgets his Lord and clings to the hem of wealth and children. In this ignorance lies his destruction, for through it, he veils the signs of his Lord and disbelieves in them. The matter has become confused for him, for his Lord is Allah, there is no god but He, the All-living (الحي), the All-sustaining (القيوم); nothing is independent of Him in any state, nor can anything avail against Him in any state.

Through this explanation, the reason for mentioning wealth (الأموال) before children (الأولاد) in the verse becomes clear. Relying on wealth—and you have seen that its origin lies in food—is more ancient in man and more familiar to him than relying on children, even if the love for children might sometimes overcome the love for wealth in a person.

The verse contains a conciseness similar to the "rebuttal of a hidden objection" (دفع الدخل). The implied meaning is: Indeed, those who disbelieve have denied Our signs and presumed that their wealth and children would avail them against Allah, but they were mistaken; for there is no sufficiency (غنى) against Allah, the Glorified, at any time or in any thing, as indicated by the following verse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **واولئك هم وقود النار** (and it is they who will be fuel for the Fire). The word *waqūd* (الوقود), with a *fathah* on the *wāw*, refers to that with which a fire is kindled and ignited. The verse follows the course of His saying, the Exalted: **فَاتَّقُوا النَّارَ الَّتِي وَقُودُهَا النَّاسُ وَالْحِجَارَةُ** — "then beware of the Fire whose fuel is humans and stones" (2:24), and His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّكُمْ وَمَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ حَصَبُ جَهَنَّمَ** — "Indeed you and what you worship besides Allah are the fuel of hell" (21:98). Some discussion regarding the meaning of this has already passed in Surah al-Baqarah.

The use of the nominal sentence (الجملة الاسمية), beginning with the demonstrative pronoun (اسم الإشارة) indicating distance, the insertion of the separating pronoun (ضمير الفصل), and the annexation of "fuel" to "the Fire" rather than saying "a fuel"—all of this emphasizes that the speech signifies exclusivity (الحصر). The implication is that the deniers among the disbelievers are the primary source of the Fire's punishment and the kindling of Hell, and that others are only burned by their fire. This is supported by what will be explained in His saying, the Exalted: **لِيَمِيزَ اللَّهُ الْخَبِيثَ مِنَ الطَّيِّبِ وَيَجْعَلَ الْخَبِيثَ بَعْضَهُ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ** — "so that Allah may separate the bad from the good, and pile up the bad on one another" (8:37).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كدأب آل فرعون والذين من قبلهم** (As was the case with Pharaoh’s clan and those who were before them) until the end of the verse. *Al-da’b* (الدأب), as mentioned by scholars, is continuous movement. The Exalted said: **وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الشَّمْسَ وَالْقَمَرَ دَائِبَيْنِ** — "And He disposed for you the sun and the moon, constant [in their courses]" (14:33). From this, a habit (العادة) is called *da’b* because it is a continuous course. This meaning is what is intended in the verse.

**His saying:** **كدأب** (As was the case) is connected to an implied word indicated by His saying in the previous verse: **لن تغني عنهم** (will not avail them). The word *da’b* is explained by His saying: **كذبوا بآياتنا** (they denied Our signs), which is in the position of a circumstantial clause (حال). The implied structure of the speech, as alluded to, is: Indeed, those who disbelieved and denied Our signs and persisted in them continuously (دائبين), presuming that in their wealth and children there was sufficiency for them against Allah, are like the case (كدأب) of the House of Pharaoh and those before them, who denied Our signs.

**His saying:** **فأخذهم الله بذنوبهم** (so Allah seized them for their sins). The letter *bā’* (الباء) apparently denotes causality (السببية); it is said, "I seized him *bi-dhanbihi*" meaning because of his sin. However, the requirement of the parallelism between the two verses—and the comparison of the state of these disbelievers in their persistence (دأبهم) to the House of Pharaoh and those before them in their persistence—is that the *bā’* denotes the instrument (الآلة). For it was mentioned regarding the disbelievers that they are the fuel of the Fire; their own selves ignite upon them and they are punished by them. Likewise, the House of Pharaoh and those before them were only seized by their sins, and the punishment that descended upon their courtyard was the very sins they committed. Their plotting was what hemmed them in, and their oppression returned upon them. The Exalted said: **وَلَا يَحِيقُ الْمَكْرُ السَّيِّئُ إِلَّا بِأَهْلِهِ** — "but the evil scheme besets only its authors" (35:43), and the Exalted said: **وَمَا ظَلَمُونَا وَلَٰكِن كَانُوا أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ** — "They did not wrong Us, but they used to wrong [only] themselves" (2:57).

From here, the meaning of His being "severe in retribution" (شديد العقاب) becomes clear. For His punishment, the Exalted, does not target a person or approach him from one direction rather than another, in one place rather than another, or upon one condition rather than another—as is the case with the punishment of others. For the evil that another human directs toward a person, for instance, only approaches him from certain directions and not others, such as from above or below, and in some places and not others; thus, it can be repelled by flight, caution, or seeking refuge. This is in contrast to the punishment of the Exalted, for He seizes man by his deed and his sin, and it is with man in his interior and exterior without ever being detached from him. He makes man the fuel for a Fire whose canopy encompasses him; neither flight nor staying still benefits him, and no escape or salvation can be found. Thus, He is severe in retribution.

In His saying, the Exalted: **كذبوا بآياتنا فأخذهم الله** (They denied Our signs, so Allah seized them), there is a transition (التفات) from the third person (الغيبة) to the second person (الحضور) first, then from the second person back to the third person. As for His saying: **كذبوا بآياتنا** (They denied Our signs), it serves to stimulate the mind of the listener and bring the report closer to truthfulness. It is as if one were to say: "So-and-so is foul-mouthed and ill-mannered, and I have been afflicted by him, so one must avoid his company." The phrase "and I have been afflicted by him" validates the report and establishes its truthfulness by referring it to direct knowledge and a kind of testimony.

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is that the House of Pharaoh were constant in the habit (دأب) of these disbelievers in disbelief and denial of the signs. There is no doubt in this report, for We were present and witnessing, and they denied *Our* signs, so *We* seized them.

As for His saying: **فأخذهم الله** (so Allah seized them), it is a return, after fulfilling the intended point, to the original mode of the speech, which is the third-person style. Furthermore, it refers the judgment back to the station of Divinity (الالوهية) which sustains all affairs of the world and dominates everything, small or great. For this reason, the Majestic Name (لفظ الجلالة) was repeated a second time in His saying: **والله شديد العقاب** (and Allah is severe in retribution), and He did not say: "and He is severe in retribution," to indicate that their disbelief and denial is a contention and a war against the One who possesses the Majesty of Divinity, for whom it is a light matter to seize the sinner for his sin. He is severe in retribution because He is Allah, majestic is His Name.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قل للذين كفروا ستغلبون وتحشرون** (Say to the faithless: ‘You will be overcome and mustered) until the end of the verse. *Al-hashr* (الحشر) is the removal of a group from their place by force; it is not used for a single individual. The Exalted said: **وَحَشَرْنَاهُمْ فَلَمْ نُغَادِرْ مِنْهُمْ أَحَدًا** — "and We shall muster them, not leaving out any one of them" (18:47). *Al-mihād* (المهاد) is the bed or resting place. The apparent context suggests that "those who disbelieve" refers to the polytheists, just as it was apparent in the previous verse: **إن الذين كفروا لن تغني عنهم** (Indeed the faithless... will not avail them), rather than the Jews. This is more appropriate for the connection between the two verses, as this verse mentions the victory over them and their being mustered to Hell, while the previous verse alluded to their self-importance and strengthening through wealth and children.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قد كان لكم آية في فئتين التقتا** (There was certainly a sign for you in the two hosts that met). The apparent context is that the address is to the disbelievers, and the speech is a continuation of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) saying: **ستغلبون وتحشرون** (You will be overcome and mustered). It is also possible that it is an address to the believers, calling them to take heed and reflect upon what Allah favored them with on the day of Badr, when He aided them with His victory—a wondrous aid through the manipulation of the vision of the eyes. According to this, the speech would contain a type of transition (التفات) by expanding the address of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) in his saying: **قل للذين** (Say to those) to include those believers with him. However, as you have seen, the first context is more appropriate.

The verse—with what it contains of the story of the meeting of the two hosts and Allah's victory for the host fighting in the way of Allah—even if it does not explicitly identify the story or name the battle, is applicable to the Battle of Badr. The Surah was revealed after it, and indeed after Uhud.

Furthermore, the verse clearly indicates that this story was well-known to the addressees with this specific detail, and they were mindful of it, as He says: **قد كان لكم آية** (There was certainly a sign for you). Allah has not narrated a story in which He mentions the manipulation of the eyes of the combatants other than the story of Badr. What He mentioned regarding the story of Badr in Surah al-Anfal: **وَإِذْ يُرِيكُمُوهُمْ إِذِ الْتَقَيْتُمْ فِي أَعْيُنِكُمْ قَلِيلًا وَيُقَلِّلُكُمْ فِي أَعْيُنِهِمْ لِيَقْضِيَ اللَّهُ أَمْرًا كَانَ مَفْعُولًا وَإِلَى اللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ الْأُمُورُ** — "And when He showed them to you, when you met, as few in your eyes, and He made you few in their eyes, so that Allah may carry out a matter that was to be fulfilled, and to Allah all matters are returned" (8:44)—even if it mentions making them appear few rather than many, it is not improbable that He made the believers appear few in the eyes of the polytheists so they would be emboldened against them and not turn away from the clash, then He made them appear many in their eyes after the meeting and engagement so they would be defeated thereby.

In any case, what is relied upon is what was in their memory regarding the increase in the eyes. Assuming the address in the verse is directed to the polytheists, the verse does not apply to any event other than the Battle of Badr. Moreover, the reading of *tarawnahum* (you see them) with the letter *tā’* also supports what we have mentioned.

The summary of the verse's meaning is: If you, O polytheists, were among those who possess sight and insight (بصيرة), it would have sufficed you as a lesson and a proof—that victory belongs to the Truth and that Allah aids with His victory whom He wills, and that one is not overcome by wealth or children—what you saw on the day of Badr. For the believers were fighting in the way of Allah, the Glorified, and they were a small, oppressed group, not even reaching a third of the disbelieving host, nor could their strength be compared to them. They were three hundred and thirteen men, possessing only six coats of mail, eight swords, and two horses. The army of the polytheists was nearly a thousand fighters, possessing equipment, strength, horses, camels, and a form that could not be measured. Yet Allah aided the believers, despite their small number and perceived weakness, against His enemies, and He made them appear numerous in their eyes, such that they saw them as twice their own number by direct vision (رأي العين). The angels aided them, and the wealth and children with which the polytheists took pride did not benefit them, nor did their gathering, large number, or strength avail them anything against Allah.

Allah, the Glorified, mentioned the case (دأب) of the House of Pharaoh and those before them in denying the signs of Allah and His seizing them for their sins in Surah al-Anfal when mentioning the story twice, just as He mentioned it here exactly.

In admonishing them by reminding them of the Battle of Badr, there is an allusion that the "overcoming" (الغلبة) intended in the previous verses is overcoming by killing and annihilation. Thus, in His signs, there is a threat of combat.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فئة تقاتل في سبيل الله وأُخری كافرة** (one host fighting in the way of Allah and another faithless). He did not say "and another in the way of Satan" or "in the way of the Taghut" and the like, because the speech is not aimed at comparing the two paths, but rather at explaining that there is no sufficiency against Allah and that victory belongs to Him. Thus, the contrast is in reality between faith in Allah and Jihad in His way, and disbelief in Allah, the Exalted.

It is apparent from the context that the two pronouns in His saying **يرونهم مثليهم** (seeing them as twice as many as themselves) refer back to His saying: **فئة تقاتل** (a host fighting), meaning the disbelieving host saw the believers as twice the number of the believers; thus they saw them as six hundred and twenty-six, while they were actually three hundred and thirteen men. As for the possibility that the two pronouns refer to different things—such that the meaning is: they saw the believers as twice the number of the disbelievers—this is far-fetched from the wording, as is obvious.

It has also been suggested that both pronouns refer to the disbelieving host, meaning: the disbelievers saw themselves doubled, twice their own number (they saw the thousand as two thousand). This would necessitate their seeing the believers as fewer in proportion; they would see them as one-sixth of themselves in number while they were actually one-third in proportion. This would be to make this verse correspond to what He mentioned in the story of Badr: **وَإِذْ يُرِيكُمُوهُمْ إِذِ الْتَقَيْتُمْ فِي أَعْيُنِكُمْ قَلِيلًا وَيُقَلِّلُكُمْ فِي أَعْيُنِهِمْ** — "And when He showed them to you, when you met, as few in your eyes, and He made you few in their eyes" (8:44), for [otherwise] one verse would contradict the other.

The response is that this leads to a confusion unsuitable for the most eloquent of speech; rather, it would have been necessary in that case to say: "they saw themselves as twice their own number" or something that conveys that. As for the contradiction between the two verses, it only occurs if the situation and station are the same, and there is no proof for that. It is possible that Allah, the Glorified, made each of the two groups appear few in the eyes of the other at the start of the meeting to strengthen their hearts and increase their boldness, until the clash began and the heat of battle intensified, at which point the disbelievers saw the believers as twice their number, causing them to be defeated and turn their backs. This is similar to His saying, the Exalted, in describing the Day of Resurrection: **لَّا يُسْأَلُ عَن ذَنبِهِ إِنسٌ وَلَا جَانٌّ** — "On that day neither human nor jinn will be questioned about his sin" (55:39), alongside His saying: **وَقِفُوهُمْ إِنَّهُم مَّسْئُولُونَ** — "And stop them [for] they are to be questioned" (37:24). This is only because one station is different from the other.

Regarding the two pronouns in His saying: **يرونهم مثليهم**, there are other possibilities mentioned by scholars, but they all share the quality of being contrary to the apparent wording; therefore, we have omitted their mention, and Allah knows best.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **والله يؤيد بنصره من يشاء ، إن في ذلك لعبرة لاولي الأبصار** (And Allah strengthens with His help whomever He wishes. There is indeed a lesson in that for those who have insight). Strengthening (التأييد) comes from *ayd* (الأيد), which is power. Regarding "those who have sight" (أولي الأبصار), it is said to refer to the physical eyes because the verse involves the manipulation of visual perception. It is also said to refer to insights (البصائر) because a lesson (العبرة) is only taken through the insight of the heart, not the physical sight. The matter is simple, for Allah, the Glorified, in His speech, considers one who does not take heed from lessons and exemplary punishments as blind. He mentions that the eye must see and distinguish truth from falsehood. In this, there is a claim that the Truth to which He calls is manifest, embodied, and sensible, such that the physical eye must see it, and that insight (البصيرة) and sight (البصر) in the realm of divine sciences are one (by way of a kind of metaphor) due to the ultimate manifestation and clarity of the Truth. The verses on this are very numerous, and among the best in indicating what we have mentioned is His saying, the Exalted: **فَإِنَّهَا لَا تَعْمَى الْأَبْصَارُ وَلَٰكِن تَعْمَى الْقُلُوبُ الَّتِي فِي الصُّدُورِ** — "For indeed it is not the eyes that turn blind, but the hearts turn blind—those that are in the breasts" (22:46), meaning that the [true] eyes are in the hearts, not the heads. Also, His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ لَّا يُبْصِرُونَ بِهَا** — "they have eyes with which they do not see" (7:179), where the verse is in a context of amazement. And His saying, the Exalted: **وَجَعَلَ عَلَىٰ بَصَرِهِ غِشَاوَةً** — "and set a covering on his sight" (45:23), among other verses. Thus, what is intended by "eyes" (الأبصار) in our present context is the physical eyes, with the claim that they are what take heed and understand. This is a form of metaphorical allusion (استعارة بالكناية), and the subtle point (النكتة) in it is the manifestation of the meaning as if it has reached the level of the senses. Its elegance is further increased by the fact that the instance involves the manipulation of the vision of the physical eye.

The apparent meaning of His saying: "Indeed, in that..." (إن في ذلك) etc., is that it is a continuation of the speech of the Exalted addressed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), and not a continuation of the Prophet's speech indicated by His saying: "Say to those who disbelieve..." (قل للذين كفروا) etc. The evidence for this is the second-person suffix (الكاف) in His saying: "that" (ذلك), for it is an address to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم). In this shift (العدول) to an address specific to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), there is an allusion (إيماء) to their lack of understanding and the blindness of their hearts, such that they fail to take heed from such lessons.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **زُيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ حُبُّ الشَّهَوَاتِ مِنَ النِّسَاءِ** — "To mankind has been made to seem decorous the love of desires, including women..." [Qarai: 3:14]. This verse and the one following it serve as an exposition (البيان) and an explanation of the reality of the situation regarding what was previously mentioned in His saying, the Exalted, just moments ago: "As for the faithless, neither their wealth nor their children will avail them anything against Allah..." [Qarai: 3:10]. This is because it appears from that verse that they believe themselves to be independent (الاستغناء) of Allah, the Glorified, through wealth and children. Thus, the verse clarifies that the cause of this is their total devotion to the love of these desired things and their being cut off by them from the matters of the Hereafter that concern them.

The matter has become confused for them, for these things are merely the enjoyment of the life of this world (متاع الحيوة الدنيا); they do not possess the quality of being a preliminary (مقدمة) for attaining the "fair resort" (حسن المآب) that is with Allah. Furthermore, they are not the originators of this love and desire, nor are they its innovators; rather, they are subjected by Divine subjugation (التسخير الإلهي) through the embedding of the root of this love within them, so that the earthly life may be completed for them. Were it not for this, the affair of the human species would not be sustained in its life and survival according to what Allah, the Glorified, has decreed for them, where He said: **وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتَاعٌ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ** — "There will be for you on the earth an abode and a provision for a while" [Qarai: 2:36].

He only decreed this for them so that they might take it as a means (وسيلة) to the Abode of the Hereafter, and take from the enjoyment of this world that which they may enjoy in that one—not so that they look at the glitter and finery of the world with an eye of independence (الاستقلال) and forget what lies beyond it, thereby taking the path (الطريق) in place of the destination (المقصد), even while they are journeying toward their Lord. The Exalted said: **إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا مَا عَلَى الْأَرْضِ زِينَةً لَّهَا لِنَبْلُوَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا وَإِنَّا لَجَاعِلُونَ مَا عَلَيْهَا صَعِيدًا جُرُزًا** — "Indeed We have made whatever is on the earth an adornment for it that We may test them [to see] which of them is best in conduct. And indeed We will turn whatever is on it into a barren waste" [Qarai: 18:7-8].

However, these heedless ones have taken these apparent Divine means—which are preliminaries and instruments (ذرائع) toward the good pleasure (رضوان) of Allah, the Glorified—as independent matters in themselves, loved for their own sake. They imagined that these things would avail them anything against Allah; thus, they became a tribulation (نقمة) upon them after having been a blessing (نعمة), and a burden (وبال) after having been a means of rewarding proximity. The Exalted said: **إِنَّمَا مَثَلُ الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا كَمَاءٍ أَنزَلْنَاهُ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ فَاخْتَلَطَ بِهِ نَبَاتُ الْأَرْضِ مِمَّا يَأْكُلُ النَّاسُ وَالْأَنْعَامُ حَتَّىٰ إِذَا أَخَذَتِ الْأَرْضُ زُخْرُفَهَا وَازَّيَّنَتْ وَظَنَّ أَهْلُهَا أَنَّهُمْ قَادِرُونَ عَلَيْهَا أَتَاهَا أَمْرُنَا لَيْلًا أَوْ نَهَارًا فَجَعَلْنَاهَا حَصِيدًا كَأَن لَّمْ تَغْنَ بِالْأَمْسِ** — "The parable of the life of this world is that of water which We send down from the sky, then the earth’s vegetation mingles with it—of which humans and cattle eat—till, when the earth puts on its luster and is adorned, and its people think they have power over it, Our edict comes to it by night or day, and We turn it into a mown field, as if it had not flourished the day before..." until He said: **وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ نَقُولُ لِلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا مَكَانَكُمْ أَنتُمْ وَشُرَكَاؤُكُمْ فَزَيَّلْنَا بَيْنَهُمْ** — "On the day We will muster them all together, then We will say to the polytheists, ‘Stay in your place, you and your partners!’ Then We will set them apart from one another..." until He said: **وَرُدُّوا إِلَى اللَّهِ مَوْلَاهُمُ الْحَقِّ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ** — "There every soul will examine what it has sent ahead, and they will be returned to Allah, their real Master, and what they used to fabricate will desert them" [Qarai: 10:24-30].

These verses indicate that the affair of life and its adornment is in His hand, the Exalted; it has no master (ولي) besides Him. Yet man, through his being deluded by its outward appearance, thinks that its affair belongs to him and that he is capable of managing and organizing it. Thus, he takes for himself partners (شركاء) therein—such as idols and what is equivalent to them of wealth, children, and other things. Indeed, Allah will make him confront his slip; He will take away this adornment and sever the ties between him and his partners. At that moment, what the human fabricated against Allah—of a partner in causal influence (التأثير)—will desert him, and the meaning and reality of what he was taught in the world will become manifest to him, and he will be returned to Allah, his real Master.

This "making seem decorous" (التزين)—meaning the appearance of the world to man with the adornment of independence (الاستقلال) and the beauty of being the ultimate goal and destination—is not attributed to Allah, the Glorified. For the All-Knowing, All-Wise Lord is far too exalted in His sanctity to manage His creation with a management (تدبير) that does not lead them to their proper end. The Exalted has said: **إِنَّ اللَّهَ بَالِغُ أَمْرِهِ** — "Indeed Allah carries through His command" [Qarai: 65:3], and the Exalted said: **وَاللَّهُ غَالِبٌ عَلَىٰ أَمْرِهِ** — "Allah has full control over His affairs" [Qarai: 12:21]. Rather, if it is to be attributed, it is attributed to Satan (الشيطان). The Exalted said: **وَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ مَا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** — "and Satan made what they were doing seem decorous to them" [Qarai: 6:43], and the Exalted said: **وَإِذْ زَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ أَعْمَالَهُمْ** — "And when Satan made their deeds seem decorous to them" [Qarai: 8:48].

Yes, for Allah, the Glorified, there is the "permission" (الإذن) in that, so that the matter of the trial (الفتنة) may be completed and the cultivation (التربية) may be established, as the Exalted said: **أَحَسِبَ النَّاسُ أَن يُتْرَكُوا أَن يَقُولُوا آمَنَّا وَهُمْ لَا يُفْتَنُونَ وَلَقَدْ فَتَنَّا الَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ فَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ صَدَقُوا وَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ الْكَاذِبِينَ أَمْ حَسِبَ الَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السَّيِّئَاتِ أَن يَسْبِقُونَا سَاءَ مَا يَحْكُمُونَ** — "Do the people suppose that they will be let off because they say, ‘We believe,’ and they will not be tested? Certainly We tested those who were before them. So Allah shall surely ascertain those who are truthful, and He shall surely ascertain the liars. Do those who commit misdeeds suppose that they can outmaneuver Us? Evil is the judgment that they make" [Qarai: 29:2-4]. Upon this permission, His saying, the Exalted, can be interpreted: **كَذَٰلِكَ زَيَّنَّا لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ عَمَلَهُمْ** — "Thus to every community We have made their conduct seem decorous" [Qarai: 6:108], although it is also possible to interpret it according to the previously mentioned meaning of adornment attributed to Him, the Exalted, in His saying: **إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا مَا عَلَى الْأَرْضِ زِينَةً لَّهَا لِنَبْلُوَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا** — "Indeed We have made whatever is on the earth an adornment for it that We may test them [to see] which of them is best in conduct" [Qarai: 18:7].

In summary, there are two types of "making seem decorous" (تزيين):
First, an adornment for the sake of using the world as a means to the Hereafter and seeking His pleasure in the various stations of life through different deeds related to wealth, status, children, and souls. This is a beautiful Divine conduct (سلوك إلهي) which Allah has attributed to Himself, as seen in the previously mentioned verse: "Indeed We have made whatever is on the earth an adornment for it..." and His saying: **قُلْ مَنْ حَرَّمَ زِينَةَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي أَخْرَجَ لِعِبَادِهِ وَالطَّيِّبَاتِ مِنَ الرِّزْقِ** — "Say, ‘Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants, and the good things of [His] provision?’" [Qarai: 7:32].

Second, an adornment to captivate hearts, making them stop at the adornment itself and distracting them from the remembrance of Allah. This is a blameworthy satanic interference (تصرف شيطاني) which Allah, the Glorified, has attributed to Satan and warned His servants against, as in the previously mentioned verse: "and Satan made what they were doing seem decorous to them," and His saying regarding what He narrates of Satan's speech: **قَالَ رَبِّ بِمَا أَغْوَيْتَنِي لَأُزَيِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ** — "He said, ‘My Lord! As You have consigned me to perversity, I will surely glamorize [evil] for them on the earth, and I will surely pervert them all’" [Qarai: 15:39], and His saying: **زُيِّنَ لَهُمْ سُوءُ أَعْمَالِهِمْ** — "the evil of their deeds has been made to seem decorous to them" [Qarai: 9:37], among other verses.

This second category is sometimes attributed to Him, the Exalted, in the sense that Satan and every cause (سبب) of good or evil only performs what it performs and acts within His kingdom by His permission (بإذنه), so that what He has willed and intended may be carried out. Through this, the order of creation and origination (الصنع والإيجاد) is organized, the successful attain victory through their good will and choice, and the criminals are distinguished.

From the preceding exposition, it becomes clear that the intended agent of the "making seem decorous" (تزيين) left unspecified in His saying: "To mankind has been made to seem decorous the love of desires..." is not Allah, the Glorified. For although the mentioned adornment has a relation to Him, the Exalted—whether it be a righteous adornment capable of inviting toward His worship, which is attributed to Him directly (بالاستقامة), or a distracting adornment that diverts from His remembrance, which is attributed to Him by permission (بالإذن)—nevertheless, because the verse contains that which is not attributed to Him directly (as will be explained), it was more appropriate to the etiquette of the Quran (أدب القرآن) that it be attributed to someone other than Him, such as Satan or the soul (النفس).

From here, the correctness of what some commentators have mentioned becomes apparent: that the agent of "made to seem decorous" (زين) is Satan, because the love of desires is a blameworthy matter, as is the love of amassing wealth; and Allah has reserved for Himself what He mentioned at the end of the verse and in what follows it.

This also reveals the error of what some have stated: that the discourse concerns human nature (طبيعة البشر) and the love arising within it, and such a thing is never attributed to Satan; rather, only that which is of the nature of whispering (الوسوسة) that makes a foul deed seem beautiful to man is attributed to him.

He [the other commentator] said: "For this reason, the Quran only attributes the adornment of deeds (الأعمال) to him. The Exalted said: 'And when Satan made their deeds seem decorous to them,' and He said: 'and Satan made what they were doing seem decorous to them.' As for realities (الحقائق) and the natures of things (طبائع الأشياء), they are only attributed to the All-Wise Creator who has no partner. The Almighty said: 'Indeed We have made whatever is on the earth an adornment for it that We may test them [to see] which of them is best in conduct,' and He said: 'Thus to every community We have made their conduct seem decorous.' Thus, the discourse regarding communities is a discourse regarding the natures of society (طبائع الاجتماع)." [End of his quote].

The reason this is erroneous is that while he was correct in saying that realities and the natures of things are only attributed to the All-Wise Creator who has no partner, he erred in saying that the discourse is about human nature and what arises from it by way of nature. This is because the Surah, as you have learned, is in the position of clarifying that Allah, the Glorified, is the Sustainer (القيوم) over His creation in all that they are upon—of creation, management, faith, disbelief, obedience, and disobedience. He created the creation and guided them to their felicity. Those who acted hypocritically in His religion, or disbelieved in His signs, or transgressed by differing over His Book—in short, those who obeyed Satan and followed their whims—do not frustrate Allah, nor do they overcome Him or invalidate His sustainment (قيمومته). Rather, everything returns to His decree and His management of His creation in establishing the law of causes (ناموس الأسباب) so that the sunnah of trial (سنة الامتحان) may be established. He is the Creator of natures, their powers, their inclinations, and their actions, so that they may be led by them to the proximity of their Lord—the proximity of nearness and honor. He is the One who gave permission to Iblis and did not prevent him from whispering and inciting, nor did He prevent man from following him by following whims, so that the matter of the trial might be completed, and so that Allah may know those who believe and take martyrs from among them.

He only clarified this in this Surah to console the souls of the believers and to comfort their hearts regarding the hardship, severity, and affliction they were facing at the time of the Surah's revelation—internally from the hypocrisy of the hypocrites and the ignorance of those in whose hearts is a sickness, who were corrupting affairs, turning things upside down for them, and falling short in obedience to Allah and His Messenger; and externally from the difficult religious call, the assault of the Arab disbelievers upon them from one side, the People of the Book (especially the Jews) from another, and the threat of disbelievers like the Romans and Persians with power and equipment from yet another side. These disbelievers and those who follow their path became confused in their reliance upon the world and its finery, as they took it as an end (غاية) when it is but a preliminary (مقدمة), and the true end lies before it.

The Surah, as you see, discusses the natures of nations (طبائع الامم), but in a broad manner that encompasses the aspects of their creation and constitution (تكوينهم) and all that follows that in the course of their lives—of traits, deeds of felicity or wretchedness, obedience or disobedience. It clarifies that all of that is under His sustainment (قيمومته), the Exalted; He is not overpowered in His power, nor overcome in His command, neither in this world nor in the Hereafter. As for this world, it is but permission and trial; as for the Hereafter, it is but recompense—if good, then good; and if evil, then evil.

Likewise, the verses—meaning His saying: "As for the faithless, neither their wealth nor their children will avail them anything..." up to the completion of nine verses—are in the position of clarifying that the disbelievers, even if they deny the signs of their Lord and change the blessings Allah bestowed upon them (which were meant to be used as means to His pleasure and His Paradise) by relying upon them, depending on them, and seeking independence through them from their Lord, and forgetting His station—they are not frustrating Him nor overcoming Him. Allah will surely seize them by their very deeds, support His faithful servants against them, and muster them to Hell, an evil resting place. Moreover, they are mistaken in relying upon what is nothing but the enjoyment of the life of this world, while with Allah is the fair resort. Thus, the verses also discuss the nature of the disbelievers, but in a broad manner that includes both the righteous and the wicked of their deeds.

Furthermore, the verse cited by this speaker as evidence that realities are only attributed to Allah while only deeds are attributed to Satan—namely His saying: "Thus to every community We have made their conduct seem decorous"—indicates the opposite of that through the surrounding context, and supports what we have mentioned. This is His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَا تَسُبُّوا الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ فَيَسُبُّوا اللَّهَ عَدْوًا بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ كَذَٰلِكَ زَيَّنَّا لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ عَمَلَهُمْ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِم مَّرْجِعُهُمْ فَيُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** — "Do not abuse those whom they invoke besides Allah, lest they should abuse Allah out of hostility, without any knowledge. Thus to every community We have made their conduct seem decorous. Then to their Lord will be their return, and He will inform them about what they used to do" [Qarai: 6:108], and this is clear.

Similarly, the error of what some have mentioned becomes apparent: that "making seem decorous" (التزيين) is of two types, praiseworthy and blameworthy, and deeds are of two types, good and evil; and only that which is praiseworthy, lauded, and good is attributed to Allah, the Glorified, while the rest belongs to Satan!

While this is true from one perspective, it is only correct regarding the direct attribution (النسبة المستقيمة) expressed by "doing" (الفعل) and the like; for Allah, the Glorified, does nothing but the beautiful, and He does not command evil or indecency. However, as for the indirect attribution (النسبة غير المستقيمة) through a medium, expressed by "permission" (الإذن) and the like, there is no barrier to it. Were it not for this, His Lordship over everything, His creation of everything, His ownership of everything, and the absolute negation of any partner for Him would not be established. The Quran is replete with this type of attribution, such as His saying: **يُضِلُّ مَن يَشَاءُ** — "He leads astray whomever He wishes" [Qarai: 13:27], and His saying: **أَزَاغَ اللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ** — "Allah made their hearts swerve" [Qarai: 61:5], and His saying: **اللَّهُ يَسْتَهْزِئُ بِهِمْ وَيَمُدُّهُمْ فِي طُغْيَانِهِمْ** — "It is Allah who mocks them, and He leaves them to flounder in their rebellion" [Qarai: 2:15], and His saying: **أَمَرْنَا مُتْرَفِيهَا فَفَسَقُوا** — "We command its luxury-loving ones, and they commit transgression in it" [Qarai: 17:16], among other verses. Their error only arose because they fell short in investigating the connections (روابط) between things, their effects, and their actions; thus, they considered each of these existing matters as an independent entity (أمراً مستقل الوجود), whose essence is severed from the collective of things and the realm of created beings that surround it, precede it, or follow it.

This necessitated that they place events—which are the results of the interaction of causes and conditions (الأسباب والعلل) according to the nature Allah instilled in them in the course of causality—as severed and scattered, neither connected nor linked. Thus, every event that occurred from its causes and every deed performed by its doer was seen as having an existence severed from others, owned by its possessor, with no share for anything other than its immediate cause in its occurrence. Thus, celestial bodies rotate, seas flow, ships sail, the earth carries, plants grow, animals crawl, and man lives and toils—with no spiritual or conceptual harmony (التيام روحي معنوي) gathering them, nor any physical unity of matter and its force unifying them.

Subsequently, they imagined a similar separation and disintegration between the conceptual titles of deeds (عناوين الأعمال) and the forms of actions—of good and evil, felicity and wretchedness, guidance and misguidance, obedience and disobedience, benevolence and malevolence, justice and injustice, and so on—as if they were not linked in existence nor intertwined in their realization.

They were oblivious to the fact that this world, with all the individual existents (أعيان الموجودات) and types of creatures it contains, is interconnected in its parts and harmonious in its portions. One part of it changes into another, and some of it transforms into others: one day a human, one day a plant, one day a mineral; one day a gathering, one day a separation. The life of some is the very death of others, and the coming into being of the new is the very corruption (فساد) of the old.

Likewise, ongoing events are linked like the links of a chain; any state assumed for one of them affects the states of what accompanies it and what preceded it back to the most ancient assumed eras of the natural world—like a chain where pulling one link pulls all the links, for it is a chain. The slightest assumed change in an atom among the atoms of this world necessitates a change of state in the whole, even if it escapes our knowledge and perception or is hidden from our senses; for lack of knowledge does not necessitate non-existence. This is something that has been clarified in scientific inquiries since ancient times, and which natural and mathematical inquiries today have elucidated most completely. Indeed, the Quran was informing us of this in the best manner before we began these inquiries—philosophical, natural, and mathematical—by transmitting from the books of others and then by independent research. This is seen in what it mentions of the continuity of management (اتصال التدبير) in the celestial and terrestrial signs, the connection between them, the benefit of some for others, the participation of all in establishing the purpose of creation, the pervasiveness of destiny (نفوذ القدر) in all of them, and the journey toward the Return (المعاد), and that "to your Lord is the finality."

Similarly, the attributes of actions and the conceptual titles of deeds are interconnected at their ends, just as the connection between opposing and conflicting matters. Were it not for one of the two conflicting sides, the affair of the other would not be established—just as we observe in the matter of creation and origination (الصنع والايجاد) that the coming into being of something requires the corruption of another, and the precedence of one matter depends on the following of another.

If one of the two sides of the attributes of deeds were not realized, the affair of the other would not be established in the effects required of it in human natural society, nor in the Divine society which is the True Religion. For obedience (الإطاعة), for example, is beautiful because disobedience (المعصية) is foul; and the good deed (الحسنة) necessitates reward because the evil deed (السيئة) necessitates punishment; and reward is pleasurable for the doer because punishment is painful for him; and pleasure is a desired felicity (سعادة) because pain is a wretchedness (شقاوة) to be fled from. Felicity is that toward which one's existence is directed according to creation, and wretchedness is that from which one is directed away. Were it not for this existential movement (الحركة الوجودية), existence itself would be nullified.

Thus, obedience (الإطاعة) followed by the righteous deed (الحسنة), then the reward (الثواب), then pleasure (اللذة), and finally felicity (السعادة) — *the ultimate state of well-being and success in this world and the Hereafter* — stands in opposition to disobedience (المعصية), followed by punishment (العقاب), then pain (الألم), and finally wretchedness (الشقاء) — *the state of ultimate misery and deprivation from divine mercy*. Each of these only manifests through the concealment of its opposite and lives through its death. How could a call toward something occur without a warning against what opposes it? And how could its opposite be possible without actually occurring through the motives and inclinations that invite toward it?

It has become clear from what we have mentioned that wisdom (الحكمة) necessitates that this world contains corruption (الفساد) just as it contains righteousness (الصلاح), and disobedience (المعصية) just as it contains obedience (الطاعة), according to what Allah has decreed in the system of His handiwork and creation. However, generation (الكون) and corruption (الفساد) in matters other than human actions and their attributes are attributed to Allah, the Glorified, because the creation (الخلق) and the command (الأمر) belong to Him alone, without any partner. As for the category of felicity (السعادة) among actions, it is attributed to Him through guidance (الهداية) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — via a direct relationship. Conversely, the category of wretchedness (الشقاوة) among them—such as the whispering (وسوسة) of Satan, the empowerment of caprice (الهوى) over man, the appointment of wrongdoers (الظالمين) as rulers over people, and the like—is attributed to Him, the Exalted, through misguidance (الإضلال), ignominy (الإخزاء), and abandonment (الخذلان) via an indirect relationship. This is what is expressed as "permission" (الإذن); thus, it is said that He, the Exalted, permitted Satan to incite through whispering and enticement, did not prevent man from following caprice, and did not place a veil between the wrongdoer and the injustice he intends. This is because felicity and wretchedness are built upon choice (الاختيار); so whoever is felicitous, it is by his choice, and whoever is wretched, it is by his choice. Were it not for this, the proof (الحجة) would not be complete, and the divine sunnah (سنة) of choice and trial (الامتحان) would not proceed.

Nothing prevented these researchers from delving excessively into these discussions except their apprehension regarding what they perceived as grim consequences. As for the proponents of predestination (المجبرة) among them, they claimed that if they were to affirm the interconnectedness of things and the necessity (ضرورة) of the causal effect of reasons (الأسباب), and were they to acknowledge that, it would necessitate compulsion (الإيجاب) on the part of the Creator (الصانع), the Exalted, and the stripping of His absolute power (القدرة المطلقة) to act within His creations.

As for others, they claimed that if they were to submit to that in the realm of actions and attribute them to the will (الإرادة) and decree (القدر) of Allah, the Exalted, it would necessitate the affirmation of compulsion (الإيجاب) and coercion (الإجبار) on the part of the created being—namely, man. By the nullification of choice (الاختيار), reward (الثواب), punishment (العقاب), religious obligation (التكليف), and legislation (التشريع) would all be nullified.

Yet, they could have found reassurance, without apprehension, in His saying, the Exalted: **وَاللَّهُ غَالِبٌ عَلَىٰ أَمْرِهِ** "And Allah has full control over His affairs" (Yusuf: 21), and His saying: **أَلَا لَهُ الْخَلْقُ وَالْأَمْرُ** "Look! To Him belong the creation and the command" (Al-A'raf: 54), and His saying: **لِلَّهِ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** "To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth" (Yunus: 55). Furthermore, these and similar verses provide the proof (البرهان) for this matter. A portion of this discussion has already preceded in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَسْتَحْيِي أَن يَضْرِبَ مَثَلًا** "Indeed Allah is not ashamed to draw a parable" (Al-Baqarah: 26).

Let us return to our previous discussion regarding His saying, the Exalted: "The love of desires... has been made to seem fair to people." We say: It appears that the agent who "made it seem fair" (زين) is someone other than Allah, the Exalted—namely, Satan or the soul (النفس). Firstly, because the context is one of censuring the disbelievers (الكفار) for their reliance on these objects of desire (المشتهيات) such as wealth and children, and their being satisfied with their adornment (تزين) to the exclusion of Allah, the Glorified. It is more appropriate that such adornment, which diverts from Allah and preoccupies one from His remembrance, should not be attributed to Him, the Exalted.

**Secondly:** If this were the adornment (التزيين) attributed to Him, the Exalted, it would mean the instinctive (الغريزي) inclination that man has toward these matters. In that case, it would have been more appropriate in expression to say: "It was made to seem fair to man (الإنسان)" or "to the children of Adam (بني آدم)" and the like, such as His saying, the Exalted: **لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنسَانَ فِي أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ ثُمَّ رَدَدْنَاهُ أَسْفَلَ سَافِلِينَ** "We certainly created man in the best of forms; then We relegated him to the lowest of the low" (At-Tin: 4-5), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ كَرَّمْنَا بَنِي آدَمَ وَحَمَلْنَاهُمْ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ** "Certainly We have honoured the Children of Adam, and carried them over land and sea..." (Al-Isra: 70). As for the term "people" (الناس), it is more commonly used in contexts involving the disregard of distinction (إلغاء الميز), the insignificance of the individual, or the baseness of thought, such as His saying: **فأبى أكثر الناس إلا كفورا** "but most people are averse to anything but ingratitude" (Al-Isra: 89), and His saying: **يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْنَاكُم مِّن ذَكَرٍ وَأُنثَىٰ** "O mankind! Indeed We created you from a male and a female" (Al-Hujurat: 13), and others.

**Thirdly:** The things Allah enumerated as an explanation of these desires do not suit primordial (الفطري) adornment. For if that were the case, it would have been more appropriate to replace the word "women" (النساء) with a term denoting the absolute concept of "spouses" (الزوجية), and the word "sons" (البنين) with "children" (الأولاد), and the word "heaps of gold and silver" (القناطير المقنطرة) with "wealth" (الأموال). This is because natural love exists in women toward men just as it exists in men toward women; likewise, it is ingrained in man toward children and wealth in general, not specifically toward "sons" or "heaps of gold and silver." Therefore, those who claim that the agent of "making it seem fair" (زين) is Allah, the Glorified, were forced to say that what is meant is the love of spouses, children, and wealth in an absolute sense, and that women, sons, and heaps were mentioned only because they are the strongest and most well-known instances; they then burdened themselves with explanations for this that have no necessity.

**Fourthly:** The fact that this adornment (التزيين) is attributed to Allah, the Glorified, does not harmonize with His saying, the Exalted, at the end of the verse: "These are the wares of the life of this world, and with Allah is a good destination. Say, 'Shall I inform you of something better than that?'" Its apparent meaning is a discourse intended to divert them from these worldly desires and direct their souls toward what is with Allah of gardens, spouses, and pleasure (الرضوان). There is no sense in diverting someone from the preliminary (المقدمة) toward the objective (ذي المقدمة) [if the preliminary itself was divinely beautified for that purpose], for that would be a manifest contradiction and a nullification of both matters together, like one who desires satiety but refrains from eating.

**If you say:** The verse—meaning His saying: "The love of desires... has been made to seem fair to people"—is, according to the summary of its meaning, equivalent to His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ مَنْ حَرَّمَ زِينَةَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي أَخْرَجَ لِعِبَادِهِ وَالطَّيِّبَاتِ مِنَ الرِّزْقِ قُلْ هِيَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا خَالِصَةً يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ** "Say, ‘Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants, and the good things of [His] provision?’ Say, ‘They are for the faithful in the life of this world, and exclusively for them on the Day of Resurrection.’" (Al-A'raf: 32). The necessary consequence of this correspondence in meaning is that the agent of adornment in this verse must also be Allah, the Glorified.

**I say:** There is a difference between the two verses in terms of context (المقام). The context in our current verse is one of censuring these desires loved by people because they divert and distract people from what they have with Allah, and urging them to turn away from them and toward what is with Allah, the Glorified. This is unlike the other verse, which is intended to explain that these blessings were adorned for man and that they belong to the believers in this world through participation, and exclusively in the Hereafter. For this reason, the term "people" (الناس) was replaced there with the term "servants" (العباد), and this adornment was counted as "good provision" (رزقاً طيباً).

**And if you say:** The adornment (التزيين) in the verse was attached to the "love of desires" (حب الشهوات) rather than the desires themselves. It is well known that making love seem fair to man, and its attraction of the self and its drawing of the heart, is a natural matter and an essential property (خاصة ذاتية) of it. Thus, the meaning of making love seem fair to people reverts to making love effective in their hearts—that is, creating (خلق) love in their hearts. Creation (الخلق) is not attributed to anyone but Allah, the Glorified; therefore, He is the agent in His saying: "made to seem fair" (زين).

**I say:** The necessary conclusion of the indicators (القرائن) we have mentioned is that the meaning of making love seem fair is to make love such that it attracts people to itself and blocks them from other than it. For adornment (الزينة) is the desired thing that attracts, which is joined to something else to draw man to that other thing by virtue of drawing him to itself—just as a woman adorns herself by joining matters that bring beauty and grace to herself so that a man may seek her through them. The object of intent is actually those matters, while the one who benefits from this seeking is the woman. In short, the meaning of making love seem fair to people reverts to making it appear in their eyes such that it leads to infatuation (التوله) with it and an avid preoccupation (الولوع) with it, not the mere principle of love's effect, as is apparent from the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: **فَخَلَفَ مِن بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ أَضَاعُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَاتَّبَعُوا الشَّهَوَاتِ فَسَوْفَ يَلْقَوْنَ غَيًّا** "Then there succeeded them an inheritance who neglected the prayer and followed [their] appetites. So they will soon encounter [the rewards of] perdition" (Maryam: 59). This meaning is supported by the upcoming discussion regarding the enumeration occurring in His saying: "of women, sons, and heaps..." Furthermore, the word "desires" (الشهوات) might not be devoid of an indication of passion (الشغف) and avidness (الولوع), even if it means the objects of desire (المشتهيات).

**His saying, the Exalted:** "of women, sons, and heaped-up heaps of gold and silver..." "Women" (النساء) is a plural that has no singular from its own root. "Sons" (البنين) is the plural of "son" (ابن), referring to male offspring, whether direct or indirect. "Heaps" (القناطير) is the plural of "qintar" (قنطار), which is a skin-bag full of gold, or simply a full skin-bag. "Heaped-up" (المقنطرة) is a passive participle (اسم مفعول) derived from "qintar," which is a non-derived noun (جامد). This is a habit of theirs [the Arabs]; they consider a sense of relationship (النسب) in non-derived nouns that grants them an infinitive meaning (معنى مصدري), and then they derive derivatives from them, such as "baqil" (باقل), "tamir" (تامر), and "attar" (عطار) for a seller of legumes (بقل), dates (تمر), and perfume (عطر). The benefit of describing a thing with an attribute taken from its own word is to solidify its meaning for it and to hint that it possesses the full meaning of its name without lacking it, as is said: "dinars made into dinars" (دنانير مدنرة) and "registers... (دواوين)"

...recorded, and it is said: a veiled veil (حجاب محجوب) and a concealed cover (ستر مستور). Horses (الخيل) refers to equines, and "marked" (المسوّمة) is derived from the pasturing of camels (سامت الإبل سوماً), meaning they went out to graze, thus they are "pasturing" (سائمة); or it is from "marking" the camels in the pasture (سمت الإبل), branding or distinguishing them. Thus, "marked horses" (الخيل المسومة) are either those sent out to pasture or those branded with a mark. Cattle (الأنعام) is the plural of *na‘am* (نعم), referring to camels, cows, and sheep. Beasts (البهائم) is more general than this and is applied to animals other than predators, birds, and insects. Tilth (الحرث) is sowing, and it carries the meaning of acquisition (الكسب), which is the cultivation of plants or the plants cultivated for use in livelihood.

The structure of the enumeration in the verse is not based on the multiplication of the love of desires according to the multiplication of the objects of desire—meaning the attachments of desire—in the sense that man, by his nature, inclines toward spouses, children, and wealth, such that one must labor to justify the expressions occurring in the verse, such as referring to man as "mankind" (الناس), or referring to children specifically as "sons" (البنين), or referring to wealth as "heaps of gold and silver" (القناطير المقنطرة), etc., as a group of exegetes have labored to do.

Rather, it is based on the fact that people are of various types in their passion and greed for the objects of worldly desire. There is the sensualist whose only concern is infatuation with women, passion for them, seeking proximity to them, and intimacy with their company; this brings with it tails of various forms of corruption and acts of disobedience to Allah, the Glorified, such as adopting musical instruments, singing, drinking intoxicants, and other matters. This is usually specific to men and is found in women only in extreme rarity. Then there is the one who loves sons, multiplication, and seeking strength through them, as is often found among desert dwellers; this is also specific to sons rather than daughters. Then there is the one infatuated with wealth, whose greatest concern is to amass heaps (يقنطر القناطير) and fill storehouses with types of currency. The manifestation of this madness for amassing wealth is primarily in the forms of currency—gold and silver—or what is valued by them, rather than furniture and the like, unless it is desired for their sake in some way; this is mostly found among urban dwellers rather than desert dwellers. Or his preference may be the acquisition of marked horses, like those infatuated with horsemanship and their like, or the acquisition of livestock from cattle, or he may prefer tilth. Sometimes some of these last three are combined with others, and sometimes they are separate.

These are the categories of desires toward which people stream, group by group, by attaching themselves to one of them and making it the primary basis for acquiring the advantages of life, while making others secondary, intended only as a second resort. Rarely is there found (or perhaps not at all) among people one who balances equally between all of them and intends all of them with a moderate, primary intention.

As for matters like prestige (الجاه), status (المقام), leadership (الصدارة), and the like, these are all in reality illusory matters (امور وهمية). Desire for them only attaches as a secondary intention; enjoying them is not considered a sensual enjoyment (شهوياً). Furthermore, the verse is not in the position of enumerating all desires exhaustively.

From here, what was previously indicated is supported: that what is meant by the "love of desires" is being deeply engrossed and immersed in their love (which is attributed to Satan), rather than the original love deposited in the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — (which is attributed to Allah, the Glorified).

**His saying, the Exalted:** ذَٰلِكَ مَتَاعُ الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا (That is the enjoyment of the life of the world). That is, these desires are matters enjoyed to sustain this life, which is the closer of the two lives to you (the worldly life and the Hereafter). The worldly life (الحيوة الدنيا), as well as the provision (المتاع) enjoyed for its sake, is a fleeting, perishing matter that has no lasting, righteous consequence. The righteousness of the end and the goodness of the return (حسن المآب) is only with Allah, the Glorified, which is His saying, the Exalted: وَاللَّهُ عِندَهُ حُسْنُ الْمَآبِ (and with Allah is the highest destination).

**His saying, the Exalted:** قُلْ أَؤُنَبِّئُكُم بِخَيْرٍ مِّن ذَٰلِكُمْ لِلَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ جَنَّاتٌ (Say, ‘Shall I inform you of something better than that? For those who are God-conscious, there are gardens with their Lord...) to the end of the verse. The verse is aimed at explaining His saying: وَاللَّهُ عِندَهُ حُسْنُ الْمَآبِ. In it, He has placed in the stead of these fleeting, vain desires matters that are better for man because they are truly lasting and good without vanity. These are matters of the same genus as these desires in terms of what man seeks of their properties and effects, except that they are free from ugliness and corruption and do not divert man from what is better than them; they are Paradise, purified spouses, and the pleasure (رضوان) of Allah, the Exalted.

Spouses (الأزواج) were specifically mentioned even though the mention of Paradise (الجنة) encompasses them, because sexual intercourse (الوقاع) is the greatest of physical pleasures for man. For this reason also, the mention of women was prioritized in His saying: مِّنَ النِّسَاءِ وَالْبَنِينَ وَالْقَنَاطِيرِ الْمُقَنطَرَةِ (of women and children, and heaped-up treasures...).

As for *ridwan* (الرضوان), with either a *kasrah* or *dammah* on the *ra*, it means pleasure (الرضا). It is when the occurring matter accords with the soul of its possessor without him refusing or repelling it; its opposite is displeasure (السخط).

The mention of Allah's pleasure (رضى الله) is repeated in the Quran. From Him, the Exalted, just as it is conceived regarding the actions of His servants in the realm of obedience, it is also conceived regarding other than the realm of obedience, such as attributes, states, and so on. However, most or all of the instances in which it is mentioned are of the type of pleasure with obedience. Therefore, it is sometimes contrasted with the pleasure of the servant: His pleasure with His servant is for his obedience, and the servant's pleasure with Him is for His beautiful reward or His decree, as in His saying, the Exalted: **رَّضِيَ اللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا عَنْهُ** — "Allah is well-pleased with them and they are well-pleased with Him" (98:8), and His saying, the Exalted: **يَا أَيَّتُهَا النَّفْسُ الْمُطْمَئِنَّةُ ارْجِعِي إِلَىٰ رَبِّكِ رَاضِيَةً مَّرْضِيَّةً** — "O soul at peace! Return to your Lord, pleased, pleasing!" (89:27-28), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَالسَّابِقُونَ الْأَوَّلُونَ مِنَ الْمُهَاجِرِينَ وَالْأَنصَارِ وَالَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوهُم بِإِحْسَانٍ رَّضِيَ اللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا عَنْهُ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ جَنَّاتٍ** — "The early vanguard of the Emigrants and the Helpers and those who followed them in virtue—Allah is well-pleased with them and they are well-pleased with Him, and He has prepared for them gardens..." (9:100).

The mention of pleasure (الرضوان) here—that is, in the list of what is better for people than the desires of the worldly life—indicates that it is itself among the desires of man or necessitates something that is so. Therefore, He intended its mention in contrast to gardens and spouses in this verse, and likewise in contrast to grace, forgiveness, and mercy in His saying: **فَضْلًا مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ وَرِضْوَانًا** — "seeking grace from their Lord and [His] pleasure" (5:2), and His saying: **وَمَغْفِرَةٌ مِّنَ اللَّهِ وَرِضْوَانٌ** — "and forgiveness from Allah and [His] pleasure" (57:20), and His saying: **بِرَحْمَةٍ مِّنْهُ وَرِضْوَانٍ** — "with mercy from Him and [His] pleasure" (9:21).

Perhaps what clarifies this matter, which this verse left ambiguous, is contemplating the meaning we mentioned and His saying, the Exalted: رضي الله عنهم (Allah is well-pleased with them) and His saying: راضية مرضية (pleased, pleasing), where He attached His pleasure to their very selves (أنفسهم). Pleasure with their selves is different from pleasure with their actions. Thus, the meaning returns to the fact that He does not bar them from Himself in what they ask, which leads to the meaning of His saying: **لَهُم مَّا يَشَاءُونَ فِيهَا** — "They will have therein whatever they wish" (50:35). Thus, in Allah's pleasure with man is the absolute will (المشية المطلقة) for man.

From here it becomes clear: that pleasure (الرضوان) in this verse is contrasted with those desires mentioned in the previous verse because man imagines that if he acquired them—especially the heaped-up treasures among them—they would grant him absolute will and give him vast power, so he would have whatever he wishes and possess whatever he wants. But the matter was confused for him; for that is only achieved through the pleasure of Allah, to Whom belongs the command of all things.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَاللَّهُ بَصِيرٌ بِالْعِبَادِ (and Allah sees the servants). Since it has been established from this verse and the one before it that Allah has prepared for man in both abodes (the world and the Hereafter) blessings to enjoy and other objectives that the soul delights in—such as spouses, food, drink, dominion, and the like—and these are similar in both abodes, except that what is in the world is shared between the disbeliever and the believer, bestowed upon both together, while what is in the Hereafter is exclusive to the believer, with the disbelievers having no share in it; this context was a place for the question of the difference in that. In other words, the question of the reason for the wisdom in restricting the believer to the blessings of the Hereafter. He answered it by saying: وَاللَّهُ بَصِيرٌ بِالْعِبَادِ. Its meaning is: that this difference by which Allah distinguished between the believer and the disbeliever is not based on futility or randomness—Exalted is He above that—but rather there is in the two groups a matter that necessitates this difference. Allah is seeing of them; He sees the difference within them, which is God-consciousness (التقوى) in the believer but not in the disbeliever. He has described this God-consciousness and defined it by what follows this verse in His saying: الَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا (Those who say, ‘Our Lord...) to the end of the two verses. The summary is: that they manifest their poverty toward their Lord and their lack of independence from Him, and they confirm that with righteous deeds. But the disbeliever deems himself independent of his Lord through the desires of the world and forgets his Hereafter and the consequence of his affair.

Among the most subtle points derived from these two verses—namely His saying, the Exalted: ذَٰلِكَ مَتَاعُ الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَاللَّهُ عِندَهُ حُسْنُ الْمَآبِ قُلْ أَؤُنَبِّئُكُم بِخَيْرٍ مِّن ذَٰلِكُمْ (That is the enjoyment of the life of the world; and with Allah is the highest destination. Say, ‘Shall I inform you of something better than that?...) to the end of the verse, and verses of similar meaning like His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ مَنْ حَرَّمَ زِينَةَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي أَخْرَجَ لِعِبَادِهِ وَالطَّيِّبَاتِ مِنَ الرِّزْقِ قُلْ هي لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا خَالِصَةً يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ كَذَٰلِكَ نُفَصِّلُ الْآيَاتِ لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ** — "Say, ‘Who has forbidden the adornment of Allah which He has brought forth for His servants, and the good things of [His] provision?’ Say, ‘These are for the faithful in the life of this world, and exclusively for them on the Day of Resurrection.’ Thus do We elaborate the signs for a people who have knowledge" (7:32)—is the answer to an objection deemed plausible by many researchers regarding the literal meanings of the verses describing the blessings of Paradise.

As for the objection: it is that whoever contemplates the modes of existence of these observed beings in this world does not doubt that the acts proceeding from them and the works they perform are branched from the powers and tools with which each of them was equipped to defend its existence and preserve its survival, as established by the study of existential ends (الغايات الوجودية) and that existence is not based on coincidence, randomness, or futility.

Behold man: he is equipped throughout his body with a precise apparatus, extremely delicate, through which the matter of his nutrition proceeds. He only nourishes himself to provide a replacement for what decomposes of his parts, and he only does that to extend his existence for survival. Also, he is equipped with the reproductive apparatus with its tools and active powers arranged to preserve the survival of his species. The matter in the existence of plants and animals is analogous to the equipment of man.

Furthermore, creation employed a ruse in subjugating them—especially the conscious ones among them, namely animals and humans—by innovating pleasures in their actions and depositing them in their powers so they would race toward the actions for the sake of these pleasures. They do not realize that creation intends through them its own end, which is the survival of existence, and it deceives them by making them covet the pleasure it beautifies for them. Thus, what creation intends is achieved, and the agent delights in this adornment that deceives and plays with him. Were it not for the pleasure in food and marriage, for example, man would not seek them merely for being a preliminary to survival, and the purpose of creation would be nullified. But Allah, the Glorified, deposited in him the pleasure of food and the pleasure of marriage; man does not rest in the path of attaining them without enduring every fatigue and toil and suffering every calamity and trial. In acquiring these desires, he is boastful and proud of what is nothing but delusion. As for the craft and creation, it attains its desire and reaches its wish; for it intended by this management nothing but the survival of the individual's existence, which was achieved by nutrition, and the survival of the species' existence, which was achieved by marriage and mating. Nothing remained for man, for example, in what he desired except the imagination.

If these worldly pleasures are intended in creation for a limited, immediate purpose, then there is no meaning for their realization where that purpose is not realized. The pleasure of eating and drinking and all pleasures related to nutrition are intended in nature for the sake of preserving the body from the affliction of decomposition and the corruption of composition, which is death. The pleasure of marriage and all pleasures associated with it—which are many matters—are only intended by creation for the sake of preserving the species from extinction and annihilation. If we assume for man an existence that is not followed by death or extinction, and a life secured from every evil and dislike, then what benefit results from the existence of bodily powers that work for the sake of achieving the survival of the person or the species? And what fruit is borne by the equipment of the body and its organs like the kidneys, bladder, spleen, liver, and others, all of which were only created for actions that benefit in the immediate, limited survival, rather than the eternal, everlasting survival?
